<findings>

[observation] [info] High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
[observation] [info] As the monorepo grows, consider grouping e2e tests into sub-directories by concern (cli/, architecture/, mcp/) to improve discoverability and allow targeted test runs.
[observation] [info] Cohesion 1.0 and zero coupling are the expected ideal for an e2e test zone — the suite is architecturally clean.
[observation] [info] cli-dev.test.js and the scheduler-startup integration test are the sole coverage gates for dev-mode startup and scheduler boot respectively; protect them from accidental skip or timeout in CI pipelines.
[pattern] [warning] cli-dev.test.js is listed in CLAUDE.md as a required test and is the sole gate for dev-mode startup coverage. If this file is skipped, flaked, or times out in CI, dev-mode regressions become undetectable. It should have an explicit timeout budget and a CI status check that alerts on skip (not just failure) to prevent silent coverage gaps.
[anti-pattern] [warning] 17 of 22 e2e test files have no documented priority status (required, sole-coverage, or disposable). Only the 5 tests named in CLAUDE.md have skip-prevention documentation. Without explicit classification, the undocumented tests are indistinguishable from expendable tests during triage, making them vulnerable to accidental exclusion in CI configuration changes.
[suggestion] [info] Add a comment block at the top of both cli-arg-contracts.test.js and cli-contract.test.js documenting the two-layer contract strategy: cli-arg-contracts guards the full public CLI surface; cli-contract guards the orchestration-spawn boundary. Without this documentation, future reviewers cannot determine whether both tests are intentional or redundant.
[suggestion] [info] Rename cli-contract.test.js to cli-orchestration-contracts.test.js (or add a describe() label clarifying its scope) to distinguish it from cli-arg-contracts.test.js. Both files test command availability but at different scopes; the naming overlap creates unnecessary ambiguity about whether both are needed.

</findings>

<insights>

- High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
- Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling confirm the suite is fully self-contained — no production code is entangled with the tests.
- With 22+ test files covering CLI delegation, architecture policy, domain isolation, and MCP transport, this suite is the primary regression net for cross-package contracts.
- Tests like cli-dev.test.js and architecture-policy.test.js are flagged in CLAUDE.md as required — any CI configuration must guarantee these run and cannot be skipped.
- Cohesion 1.0 and zero coupling are the expected ideal for an e2e test zone — the suite is architecturally clean.
- cli-dev.test.js and the scheduler-startup integration test are the sole coverage gates for dev-mode startup and scheduler boot respectively; protect them from accidental skip or timeout in CI pipelines.
- As the monorepo grows, consider grouping e2e tests into sub-directories by concern (cli/, architecture/, mcp/) to improve discoverability and allow targeted test runs.
- The e2e zone's architecture-policy.test.js and domain-isolation.test.js use file-system and spawn-based assertions rather than import-level checks. This means violations are caught post-build during CI but are invisible to TypeScript tooling at development time. The integration between these two enforcement strategies (compile-time types + runtime e2e assertions) is the monorepo's primary defense against architecture regression.
- cli-dev.test.js is listed in CLAUDE.md as a required test and is the sole gate for dev-mode startup coverage. If this file is skipped, flaked, or times out in CI, dev-mode regressions become undetectable. It should have an explicit timeout budget and a CI status check that alerts on skip (not just failure) to prevent silent coverage gaps.
- 17 of 22 e2e test files have no documented priority status (required, sole-coverage, or disposable). Only the 5 tests named in CLAUDE.md have skip-prevention documentation. Without explicit classification, the undocumented tests are indistinguishable from expendable tests during triage, making them vulnerable to accidental exclusion in CI configuration changes.
- cli-arg-contracts.test.js and cli-contract.test.js test overlapping concerns (both verify CLI command availability) but with different scopes: the former validates the full public CLI surface, the latter validates only the subset invoked by the orchestration layer. The names do not convey this scope distinction — a reader cannot determine which is narrower without reading the file content. This is a naming inconsistency that will cause future maintainers to question whether both files are needed or if one is a duplicate.
- cli-arg-contracts.test.js and cli-contract.test.js represent two complementary contract layers: public API integrity (all commands exist) vs orchestration spawn integrity (invoked commands exist). This two-layer contract strategy is an implicit convention not documented in CLAUDE.md or in the test files themselves — capturing it as a comment or test suite description would prevent redundancy concerns from triggering premature consolidation.
- Rename cli-contract.test.js to cli-orchestration-contracts.test.js (or add a describe() label clarifying its scope) to distinguish it from cli-arg-contracts.test.js. Both files test command availability but at different scopes; the naming overlap creates unnecessary ambiguity about whether both are needed.
- Add a comment block at the top of both cli-arg-contracts.test.js and cli-contract.test.js documenting the two-layer contract strategy: cli-arg-contracts guards the full public CLI surface; cli-contract guards the orchestration-spawn boundary. Without this documentation, future reviewers cannot determine whether both tests are intentional or redundant.
- [call graph] 469 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

</insights>
